title: Witch Week - Chapter 11 (Fragment 1)
text:
11

11

C HARLES JOGGED around the playing field towards the groundsman’s hut. He hoped anyone who saw him would think he was out running for PE. For this reason, he had changed into his small sky-blue running shorts before he slipped away. When he had time, he supposed he could transform the shorts into jeans or something. But the important thing at the moment was to get hold of that mangy old broom people had been taunting Nan Pilgrim with the other day. If he got to that before anyone noticed he was missing, he could ride away on it and no dog on earth could pick up his trail.

He reached the hut, in its corner beside the kitchen gardens. He crept around it to its door. At the same moment, Nirupam crept around it from the opposite side, also in sky-blue shorts, and stretched out his long arm for the door too. The two of them stared at one another. All sorts of ideas for things to say streamed through Charles’s mind, from explaining he was just dodging PE, to accusing Nirupam of kidnapping Brian. In the end, he said none of these things. Nirupam had hold of the doorlatch by then.

“Bags I the broomstick,” Charles said.

“Only if there are two of them,” said Nirupam. His face was yellow with fear. He pulled open the door and dodged into the hut. Charles dived in after him.

There was not even the one old broomstick. There were flowerpots, buckets, an old roller, a new roller, four rakes, two spades, a hoe, and an old wet mop propped in one of the buckets. And that was all.

“Who took it?” Charles said wildly.

“Nobody brought it back,” said Nirupam.

“Oh, magic it all!” said Charles. “What shall we do ?”

“Use something else,” said Nirupam. “Or walk.” He seized the nearest spade and stood astride it, bending and stretching his great long legs. “Fly,” he told the spade. “Go on, fly, magic you!”

Nirupam had the right idea, Charles saw. A witch surely ought to be able to make anything fly. “I should think a rake would fly better,” he said, and quickly grabbed hold of the wet mop for himself. The mop was so old that it had stuck to the bucket. Charles was forced to put one foot on the bucket and pull, before it came loose, and a lot of the head got left behind in the bucket. The result was a stick ending in a scraggly gray stump. Charles seized it and stood astride it. He jumped up and down. “Fly!” he told the mop. “Quick!”

Nirupam threw the spade down and snatched up the hoe. Together they jumped desperately around the hut. “Fly!” they panted. “Fly!”

In an old, soggy, dispirited way, the mop obeyed. It wallowed up about three feet into the air and wove towards the hut door. Nirupam was wailing in despair, when the hoe took off too, with a buck and a rush, as if it did not want to be left behind. Nirupam shot past Charles with his huge legs flailing. “It works!” he panted triumphantly, and went off in another kangaroo bound towards the kitchen gardens.

They were forbidden to go in the kitchen gardens, but it seemed the most secret way out of school. Charles followed Nirupam through the gate and down the gravel path, both of them trying to control their mounts. The mop wallowed and wove. It was like an old, old person, feebly hobbling through the air. The hoe either went by kangaroo surges, or it slanted and trailed its metal end along the path. Nirupam had to stick his feet out in front in order not to leave a scent on the ground. His eyes rolled in agony. He kept overtaking Charles and dropping behind. When they got to the wall at the end of the garden, both implements stopped. The mop wallowed about in the air. The hoe jittered its end on the gravel.

vocabulary.definition: The wooden handle of a broom.
vocabulary.example: “Bags I the broomstick,” Charles said.

vocabulary.definition: A container with an open top and handle used for carrying liquids or materials.
vocabulary.example: There were flowerpots, buckets, an old roller, a new roller, four rakes, two spades, a hoe, and an old wet mop propped in one of the buckets.

vocabulary.definition: To exercise authority or influence over something.
vocabulary.example: Charles followed Nirupam through the gate and down the gravel path, both of them trying to control their mounts.

vocabulary.definition: A point or area where two edges or sides meet.
vocabulary.example: He reached the hut, in its corner beside the kitchen gardens.

vocabulary.definition: To move slowly and quietly in order not to be noticed.
vocabulary.example: He crept around it to its door.

vocabulary.definition: In a way that shows despair or a great need for something.
vocabulary.example: Together they jumped desperately around the hut.

vocabulary.definition: Having lost enthusiasm and hope; discouraged.
vocabulary.example:
”

In an old, soggy, dispirited way, the mop obeyed.

vocabulary.definition: To avoid something by a sudden quick movement.
vocabulary.example: All sorts of ideas for things to say streamed through Charles’s mind, from explaining he was just dodging PE, to accusing Nirupam of kidnapping Brian.

vocabulary.definition: A container in which flowers and plants are grown.
vocabulary.example: There were flowerpots, buckets, an old roller, a new roller, four rakes, two spades, a hoe, and an old wet mop propped in one of the buckets.

vocabulary.definition: Not allowed; banned.
vocabulary.example: They were forbidden to go in the kitchen gardens, but it seemed the most secret way out of school.

vocabulary.definition: Small stones used for paths, roads, or as a surface layer.
vocabulary.example: Charles followed Nirupam through the gate and down the gravel path, both of them trying to control their mounts.

vocabulary.definition: To walk with difficulty, typically because of pain from an injury.
vocabulary.example: It was like an old, old person, feebly hobbling through the air.

vocabulary.definition: A gardening tool with a thin blade set at a right angle to a handle, used for weeding and breaking up soil.
vocabulary.example: There were flowerpots, buckets, an old roller, a new roller, four rakes, two spades, a hoe, and an old wet mop propped in one of the buckets.

vocabulary.definition: A tool, utensil, or other piece of equipment used for a particular purpose.
vocabulary.example: When they got to the wall at the end of the garden, both implements stopped.

vocabulary.definition: To make small rapid movements; to tremble or shake.
vocabulary.example: The hoe jittered its end on the gravel.

vocabulary.definition: To breathe quickly and heavily, usually after exertion.
vocabulary.example: ” they panted.

vocabulary.definition: A tool with a long handle and teeth used to gather leaves or smooth soil.
vocabulary.example: There were flowerpots, buckets, an old roller, a new roller, four rakes, two spades, a hoe, and an old wet mop propped in one of the buckets.

vocabulary.definition: A cylindrical tool used for smoothing or flattening surfaces.
vocabulary.example: There were flowerpots, buckets, an old roller, a new roller, four rakes, two spades, a hoe, and an old wet mop propped in one of the buckets.

vocabulary.definition: Growing or hanging in an untidy way; irregular and uneven.
vocabulary.example: The result was a stick ending in a scraggly gray stump.

vocabulary.definition: Kept hidden from others; private.
vocabulary.example: They were forbidden to go in the kitchen gardens, but it seemed the most secret way out of school.

vocabulary.definition: A tool with a flat blade and a long handle used for digging.
vocabulary.example: There were flowerpots, buckets, an old roller, a new roller, four rakes, two spades, a hoe, and an old wet mop propped in one of the buckets.

vocabulary.definition: To provoke or challenge someone with insulting remarks.
vocabulary.example: But the important thing at the moment was to get hold of that mangy old broom people had been taunting Nan Pilgrim with the other day.

vocabulary.definition: A path or track left behind by a person, animal, or object moving through an area.
vocabulary.example: If he got to that before anyone noticed he was missing, he could ride away on it and no dog on earth could pick up his trail.

vocabulary.definition: To move or roll about in an ungainly or clumsy way.
vocabulary.example: It wallowed up about three feet into the air and wove towards the hut door.

vocabulary.definition: To move by twisting and turning in and out.
vocabulary.example: It wallowed up about three feet into the air and wove towards the hut door.
